bank: Headline with a question form has more clicks than headline without a question form
Headline containing ellipses or trailing dots has more clicks than headline without ellipses
Headline with phrases implying missing information (e.g., 'You won't believe') has more clicks than headline without such phrases
Headline with higher count of emotionally charged words (positive or negative) has more clicks than headline with fewer such words
Headline with simpler vocabulary and lower average word length has more clicks than headline with complex vocabulary
Headline with lower syntactic complexity has more clicks than headline with higher syntactic complexity
Headline with better readability scores (easier to read) has more clicks than headline with poorer readability
Headline using second-person pronouns ('you') has more clicks than headline without second-person pronouns
Headline with direct calls to action (e.g., 'Discover', 'Find out') has more clicks than headline lacking calls to action
Headline written in active voice has more clicks than headline written in passive voice
Headline containing novel or uncommon words has more clicks than headline with only common words
Headline that evokes positive emotional valence has more clicks than headline with neutral or negative valence
Headline that evokes negative emotional valence has more clicks than headline with neutral or positive valence
Headline that implies urgency or scarcity (e.g., 'Now', 'Limited time') has more clicks than headline lacking urgency cues
Headline that is shorter in length (fewer words) has more clicks than headline that is longer
